Notice Of Motion And Motion For Terminating Sanctions
Off Calendar Feb-05-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Peter Makras and Kari Makras (707-449-1899) Plaintiffs in propria persona.
Lisa T. Ungerer, esq. (510-465-3922) of Rankin, Sproat, Mires...APC for Defendants.

ARGUED. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANTs ERIC HANSEN, BRENDA STENGELE, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA's Motion For Terminating Sanctions

Defendants' motion is granted in part and denied in part as follows: The court declines to award terminating sanctions, which may only be awarded for discovery abuse "(t)o the extent authorized by the chapter governing any particular discovery method."(CCP 2023.030.) The chapter governing requests for admission does not authorize terminating sanctions. (See CCP section 2033.290) However, plaintiffs have disobeyed the court's August 8, 2018 order to serve Code compliant verified further responses to defendants' Requests for Admission, Set Two, Nos. 14-22 to Peter Makras and Requests for Admission, Set Two, Nos. 10-20 to Kari Makras. Therefore the matters involved in these requests for admission are hereby deemed admitted pursuant to CCP section 2033.290(e) and defendants' related motion. In regard to plaintiffs' disobeying the August 8, 2018 order to serve objection-free, complete and verified responses to defendants' Requests for Production of Documents, Set Four, the court declines to award terminating sanctions because due process requires that sanction awards go no further than is necessary to accomplish the purpose of discovery. (Newland v. Sup. Ct. (1995) 40 Cal.App.4th, 608, 613; Caryl Richards, Inc. v. Sup. Ct. (1961) 188 Cal.App.2d, 300, 305.) That purpose is accomplished here as to document request Nos. 46 - 54 and 61 by granting evidentiary sanctions to preclude plaintiffs from introducing any further documents into evidence that were not already produced by August 8, 2018 related to loss of income; payments by Medicare; payments by Medi-Cal; and plaintiffs' alleged contacts with defendants. As to document request Nos. 55-60 and 62, an issue preclusion sanction is granted to preclude plaintiffs from contesting that in a lawsuit they filed against the 3M Company plaintiffs claim Mr. Makras' leg injuries were caused by the Bair Hugger surgical warming device.

Order is signed.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares his views to parties. Defendant states history of case and cites case law. The Court encourages party to file a writ and overrules Mr. & Mrs. Makra's evidentiary objections. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)
The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument changes the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ERIC HANSEN, BRENDA STENGELE, THE REGENTS OF THE UNIVERSITY OF CALIFORNIA'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT.

Judge Kahn discloses that the General Counsel of the University of California is a close friend of his, but neither that friendship nor any other matter causes him to be other than fair and impartial in deciding this case. Defendant The Regents of the University of California and Eric Hansen M.D.'s motion for summary judgment is denied. There is a triable issue whether defendants breached their duty of care and caused plaintiff Peter Makras' alleged harm. (Lundy Dec., 7:1-10:28; Peter Makras Depo., 83:6-84:7; Kari Makras Depo., 149:1-150:12.) While ordinarily a plaintiff needs to submit a declaration from a medical expert to defeat a summary judgment motion in a medical malpractice case, that general rule is inapplicable to this motion because of the asserted admissions by Dr. Hansen.(as fully reported in the reporter's transcript)

The Motion for sumary Judgment is granted as to Defendant Brenda Stengele.

Order is signed in Open Court. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Ann Delgado, CSR #11185, email:ADelgado@sftc.org, Reported. (D302)
